does: This paper addresses catastrophic failures in autoregressive neural-codec text-to-speech models—where systems produce silence, early termination, repetitive content, or hallucinations on a meaningful fraction of utterances. The authors develop a two-stage approach: first, they use automatic speech recognition (ASR) round-trip verification to identify and filter failures through best-of-N sampling, achieving near-zero catastrophic failure rates (N=2 on LibriSpeech, N=4 on hard prompts). Second, they distill this reliable behavior back into the base model via knowledge distillation, recovering approximately 52-58% of the robustness gain at inference time without requiring multiple samples. The method is demonstrated across four codec-TTS systems and three neural codecs (XCodec2, SNAC, Mimi), showing the approach generalizes robustly across architectures while incurring no computational cost during deployment.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in neural codec-based TTS systems that achieve high naturalness through autoregressive generation over discrete codes. It extends the broader quality-assurance literature in neural text generation by adapting ASR verification—a technique borrowed from speech processing—to detect and eliminate failure modes in generative models. The paper connects to distillation methods used to compress multi-pass inference into single-pass models, as well as to sampling-based reliability techniques like best-of-N decoding. The catastrophic failure problem it addresses is a known but under-explored issue in open-source codec-TTS models, and the work responds to the gap between impressive benchmark performance and brittleness on diverse real-world inputs, drawing on insights from robustness and failure-mode analysis in deep learning.
enables: This work enables reliable deployment of neural-codec TTS in production systems by providing a practical, zero-cost solution to a previously intractable failure mode. The approach could generalize to other generative speech and language models suffering from similar stochastic collapse behaviors, suggesting broader applications in robustifying autoregressive models beyond TTS. The ASR self-verification metric establishes a reusable format-agnostic evaluation framework for measuring catastrophic failures, potentially informing how the community assesses reliability of generative models. The distillation pipeline demonstrates how to transfer robustness from expensive inference-time verification to lightweight single-pass models, opening pathways for making other multi-step verification schemes practical. Future work could explore whether similar self-verification and distillation strategies apply to other failure modes in speech and language generation, or whether the method scales to even harder distribution shifts.
